section.title: Laws About Skin Diseases
section.sectionRationale: This long chapter gives the priest's diagnostic procedure for skin diseases affecting people, and then extends the same principle of examination to mildew in fabric and leather, continuing the purity regulations with detailed, careful case law entrusted to the priesthood.
section.thesis: God entrusts the priests with the careful, repeated examination of skin conditions and contaminated fabric, teaching that some matters affecting the community's holiness cannot be judged quickly or by appearance alone, but require patient, authoritative discernment before a person or object is declared clean or unclean.
section.frameNote: Four movements: diagnosing suspicious skin conditions through repeated examination; cases of chronic disease, boils, burns, and scalp disorders; the diseased person's isolation and public cry; and the parallel law for mildew contaminated fabric and leather.

verses.text: Then the LORD said to Moses and Aaron,
“When someone has a swelling or rash or bright spot on his skin that could become an infectious skin disease, he must be brought to Aaron the priest or to one of his sons who is a priest.

The priest is to examine the infection on his skin, and if the hair in the infection has turned white and the sore appears to be deeper than the skin, it is a skin disease. After the priest examines him, he must pronounce him {{tame|unclean}}.
If, however, the spot on his skin is white and does not appear to be deeper than the skin, and the hair in it has not turned white, the priest shall isolate the infected person for seven days.
On the seventh day the priest is to reexamine him, and if he sees that the infection is unchanged and has not spread on the skin, the priest must isolate him for another seven days.

The priest will examine him again on the seventh day, and if the sore has faded and has not spread on the skin, the priest shall pronounce him {{tahor|clean}}; it is a rash. The person must wash his clothes and be clean.
But if the rash spreads further on his skin after he has shown himself to the priest for his cleansing, he must present himself again to the priest.
The priest will reexamine him, and if the rash has spread on the skin, the priest must pronounce him unclean; he has a skin disease.
When anyone develops a skin disease, he must be brought to the priest.
The priest will examine him, and if there is a white swelling on the skin that has turned the hair white, and there is raw flesh in the swelling,
it is a chronic skin disease and the priest must pronounce him unclean. He need not isolate him, for he is unclean.
But if the skin disease breaks out all over his skin so that it covers all the skin of the infected person from head to foot, as far as the priest can see,
the priest shall examine him, and if the disease has covered his entire body, he is to pronounce the infected person clean. Since it has all turned white, he is clean.
But whenever raw flesh appears on someone, he will be unclean.
When the priest sees the raw flesh, he must pronounce him unclean. The raw flesh is unclean; it is a skin disease.
But if the raw flesh changes and turns white, he must go to the priest.
The priest will reexamine him, and if the infection has turned white, the priest is to pronounce the infected person clean; then he is clean.
When a boil appears on someone's skin and it heals,
and a white swelling or a reddish-white spot develops where the boil was, he must present himself to the priest.

The priest shall examine it, and if it appears to be beneath the skin and the hair in it has turned white, the priest shall pronounce him unclean; it is a diseased infection that has broken out in the boil.
But when the priest examines it, if there is no white hair in it, and it is not beneath the skin and has faded, the priest shall isolate him for seven days.
If it spreads any further on the skin, the priest must pronounce him unclean; it is an infection.
But if the spot remains unchanged and does not spread, it is only the scar from the boil, and the priest shall pronounce him clean.
When there is a burn on someone's skin and the raw area of the burn becomes reddish-white or white,

the priest must examine it. If the hair in the spot has turned white and the spot appears to be deeper than the skin, it is a disease that has broken out in the burn. The priest must pronounce him unclean; it is a diseased infection.
But if the priest examines it and there is no white hair in the spot, and it is not beneath the skin but has faded, the priest shall isolate him for seven days.
On the seventh day the priest is to reexamine him, and if it has spread further on the skin, the priest must pronounce him unclean; it is a diseased infection.
But if the spot is unchanged and has not spread on the skin but has faded, it is a swelling from the burn, and the priest is to pronounce him clean; for it is only the scar from the burn.

units.label: Scalp, bald spots, and the diseased person's cry

verses.text: If a man or woman has an infection on the head or chin,

the priest shall examine the infection, and if it appears to be deeper than the skin and the hair in it is yellow and thin, the priest must pronounce him unclean; it is a scaly outbreak, an infectious disease of the head or chin.
But if the priest examines the scaly infection and it does not appear to be deeper than the skin, and there is no black hair in it, the priest shall isolate the infected person for seven days.
On the seventh day the priest is to reexamine the infection, and if the scaly outbreak has not spread and there is no yellow hair in it, and it does not appear to be deeper than the skin,
then the person must shave himself except for the scaly area. Then the priest shall isolate him for another seven days.

On the seventh day the priest shall examine the scaly outbreak, and if it has not spread on the skin and does not appear to be deeper than the skin, the priest is to pronounce him clean. He must wash his clothes, and he will be clean.
If, however, the scaly outbreak spreads further on the skin after his cleansing,
the priest is to examine him, and if the scaly outbreak has spread on the skin, the priest need not look for yellow hair; the person is unclean.
If, however, in his sight the scaly outbreak is unchanged and black hair has grown in it, then it has healed. He is clean, and the priest is to pronounce him clean.
When a man or a woman has white spots on the skin,
the priest shall examine them, and if the spots are dull white, it is a harmless rash that has broken out on the skin; the person is clean.
Now if a man loses his hair and is bald, he is still clean.
Or if his hairline recedes and he is bald on his forehead, he is still clean.
But if there is a reddish-white sore on the bald head or forehead, it is an infectious disease breaking out on it.
The priest is to examine him, and if the swelling of the infection on his bald head or forehead is reddish-white like a skin disease,
the man is diseased; he is unclean. The priest must pronounce him unclean because of the infection on his head.
A diseased person must wear torn clothes and let his hair hang loose, and he must cover his mouth and cry out, ‘Unclean, unclean!’
As long as he has the infection, he remains unclean. He must live alone in a place outside the camp.
If any fabric is contaminated with mildew, any wool or linen garment,
any weave or knit of linen or wool, or any article of leather,
and if the mark in the fabric, leather, weave, knit, or leather article is green or red, then it is contaminated with mildew and must be shown to the priest.
And the priest is to examine the mildew and isolate the contaminated fabric for seven days.

On the seventh day the priest shall reexamine it, and if the mildew has spread in the fabric, weave, knit, or leather, then regardless of how it is used, it is a harmful mildew; the article is unclean.
He is to burn the fabric, weave, or knit, whether the contaminated item is wool or linen or leather. Since the mildew is harmful, the article must be burned up.
But when the priest reexamines it, if the mildew has not spread in the fabric, weave, knit, or leather article,
the priest is to order the contaminated article to be washed and isolated for another seven days.

After it has been washed, the priest is to reexamine it, and if the mildewed article has not changed in appearance, it is unclean. Even though the mildew has not spread, you must burn it, whether the rot is on the front or back.
If the priest examines it and the mildew has faded after it has been washed, he must cut the contaminated section out of the fabric, leather, weave, or knit.
But if it reappears in the fabric, weave, or knit, or on any leather article, it is spreading. You must burn the contaminated article.
If the mildew disappears from the fabric, weave, or knit, or any leather article after washing, then it is to be washed again, and it will be clean.
This is the law concerning a mildew contamination in wool or linen fabric, weave, or knit, or any leather article, for pronouncing it clean or unclean.”

tame.gloss: Unclean, a status pronounced only by the priest after careful examination, never assumed or self declared. This chapter shows that some judgments about holiness and community life require patient, authoritative discernment, not snap conclusions.

tahor.gloss: Clean, the status restored, likewise only through the priest's authoritative pronouncement after examination. The repeated procedure of isolation and reexamination shows that being declared clean was a real, careful process, not a formality.

basic.context: This long chapter gives detailed instructions for priests diagnosing suspicious skin conditions, sometimes traditionally called leprosy though covering a range of skin diseases, through repeated examination over days and weeks rather than a single glance. It addresses chronic disease, boils, burns, scalp conditions, and baldness, before extending the same pattern of examination to mildew contaminating fabric or leather.
basic.hermeneutics: This gives Israel's actual old covenant procedure for diagnosing and managing skin disease and contaminated material; it is not a medical manual, nor does it prescribe ritual quarantine practices for the church today. Its abiding truth is that some matters affecting a community require patient, authoritative, and careful discernment rather than hasty judgment, and that what threatens the community's health, physical or spiritual, must be neither ignored nor treated with panic.
basic.originalLanguages: The priest alone could pronounce someone tame, unclean, or tahor, clean, and always only after careful, sometimes repeated examination over a full week or more. This careful process shows that these judgments were never left to snap decisions, self diagnosis, or public rumor, but to trained, authoritative, patient discernment.
basic.typology: The diseased person's required cry, unclean, unclean, and enforced isolation outside the camp foreshadow the isolating, alienating power of sin, while the priest's careful examination anticipates the discernment Christ Himself, and later the church, would need in matters of sin and restoration, Matthew 18:15-17. Jesus' willingness to touch lepers and heal them, Mark 1:40-42, directly reverses the isolation this chapter required.
basic.christ: Where this law required the diseased to be isolated and declared unclean, Jesus reached out and touched lepers, making them clean rather than becoming unclean Himself, Mark 1:40-42, reversing the direction of contagion entirely. Christ's holiness is not defeated by contact with defilement; instead, His touch cleanses, foreshadowing the greater cleansing He provides for sin itself.
basic.god: God's law reveals real care for the health and holiness of the community, requiring careful, patient examination rather than either denial or panic when something threatens it. Yet the same God who required isolation for these diseases sent His own Son to touch the untouchable, showing that exclusion was never His final word for those marked by disease or defilement.
basic.crossReferences: Mark 1:40-42 shows Jesus touching a leper and healing him, reversing the direction of defilement this chapter describes. Matthew 18:15-17 gives the church a careful, step by step process for addressing sin in the community, echoing this chapter's patient, non hasty discernment. 2 Kings 5 tells of Naaman's healing from leprosy, showing God's power to cleanse even a foreign commander of this very disease.
basic.oneStory: This chapter shows God requiring patient, authoritative discernment before declaring someone clean or unclean, never leaving such judgments to rumor or hasty assumption. It anticipates Christ, who reverses the isolating power of disease and defilement entirely, reaching out to touch and heal those this very law once required to be cast out.

context.literary: This chapter continues the purity regulations begun with food laws in chapter eleven and childbirth in chapter twelve, now addressing the human body's own skin, before its companion chapter fourteen provides the cleansing ritual for those declared clean.
context.historical: The skin diseases described here, often traditionally translated leprosy, covered a range of conditions broader than what is medically called Hansen's disease today; the priest's role was diagnostic and communal, not primarily medical treatment.
context.canonical: This chapter's procedures form the background for the Gospel accounts of Jesus healing lepers, whose restoration to community required a priest's declaration exactly as prescribed here, Mark 1:44, and for the broader biblical pattern connecting skin disease to themes of sin, judgment, and restoration, as with Miriam in Numbers 12 and Naaman in 2 Kings 5.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To establish a careful, patient, priestly procedure for diagnosing and managing skin disease and mildew contamination, protecting the community's health and holiness while ensuring judgments of unclean or clean were made only after thorough, repeated examination.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This prescribes actual diagnostic and quarantine procedures for old covenant Israel; it is not a medical or ceremonial code binding on the church today. Its abiding principle is that matters affecting a community's health and holiness deserve patient, careful discernment rather than hasty judgment or careless denial.

debates.question: Does the Hebrew term traditionally translated leprosy in this chapter refer to what is medically known today as Hansen's disease?

views.label: A broader category of skin and fabric conditions
views.case: Most conservative scholars now recognize that the Hebrary tsara'at describes a range of skin conditions, discolorations, and even fabric or house mildew, broader than the specific modern medical diagnosis of Hansen's disease, since the same term applies to garments and houses in chapters 13 and 14.

views.label: Including some cases of what is now called leprosy
views.case: Others maintain that at least some cases described, particularly the chronic, spreading conditions, may include what is medically recognized as leprosy today, without excluding the broader range of conditions the term also covers.

debates.bearing: Both views agree the text describes a real, communicable concern requiring careful management, whatever its precise modern medical classification.

study.originalLanguages: Only the priest could pronounce someone tame, unclean, or tahor, clean, and this pronouncement came only after careful, sometimes repeated examination extending over a week or more, never a snap judgment. This process protected the community from both the danger of ignoring a genuine threat and the danger of hasty, wrongful condemnation.

typology.type: The diseased person's cry, unclean, unclean, and isolation outside the camp
typology.fulfillment: The isolating power of sin, reversed when Jesus touches and heals lepers rather than becoming unclean Himself, Mark 1:40-42.

typology.type: The priest's patient, careful examination before declaring clean or unclean
typology.fulfillment: The careful, step by step process the church is given for addressing sin within the community, Matthew 18:15-17.

study.christ: Mark 1:40-42 records Jesus touching a leper, an act that under this law would have made Jesus Himself unclean, yet instead the leper is healed and Jesus remains undefiled, reversing entirely the direction defilement was assumed to flow. Christ's willingness to draw near to the very people this chapter required to be isolated anticipates His greater work of drawing near to sinners generally, touching what was unclean and making it whole.
study.god: God's law shows real concern for the community's health and holiness, refusing to let either denial or panic dictate the response to disease; instead it commands patient, repeated examination. Yet this same God, in Christ, ultimately reaches across the very boundary this law established, showing that exclusion, however necessary for a season, was never His final intention for those it isolated.

crossReferences.note: Jesus touches a leper and heals him, reversing the direction of defilement this chapter assumes.
The church is given a careful, step by step process for addressing sin, echoing this chapter's patient discernment before pronouncing judgment.
Naaman, a foreign commander, is healed of his skin disease, showing God's power to cleanse even beyond Israel's borders.

study.oneStory: This chapter entrusts priests with patient, careful discernment before declaring someone unclean or clean, refusing both hasty condemnation and careless denial of real danger. It anticipates Christ, who reaches out to touch and heal those this very law once required to be isolated, reversing the flow of defilement and showing that exclusion was never God's final word.

quiz.q: What must happen when a suspicious skin spot is ambiguous, neither clearly diseased nor clearly harmless?

options.text: The priest isolates the person for seven days, then reexamines before making a judgment.
options.note: Verses 4 to 6 describe this careful process of isolation and reexamination for ambiguous cases.

options.text: The priest must immediately declare the person unclean to be safe.
options.note: The text describes patient reexamination for ambiguous cases, not an immediate default to uncleanness.

options.text: The person may decide for themselves whether they are clean.
options.note: Only the priest, after examination, may pronounce someone clean or unclean, not the individual themselves.

options.text: No process is given for ambiguous cases.
options.note: Verses 4 to 6 give a detailed process for exactly this situation.

quiz.q: According to verses 12 to 13, what happens if a skin disease covers a person's entire body from head to foot?

options.text: The priest pronounces the person clean, since it has all turned white.
options.note: Verse 13 gives this counterintuitive but explicit ruling.

options.text: The person is immediately and permanently unclean.
options.note: Verse 13 explicitly states the opposite outcome for this specific case of complete coverage.

options.text: The person must be quarantined for life.
options.note: The text specifies a declaration of clean in this particular case, not lifelong quarantine.

options.text: No ruling is given for this case.
options.note: Verses 12 to 13 give an explicit ruling.

quiz.q: What must a diseased person do while their infection persists, according to verses 45 to 46?

options.text: Wear torn clothes, let their hair hang loose, cover their mouth, cry out 'Unclean, unclean,' and live alone outside the camp.
options.note: Verses 45 to 46 give this exact, detailed requirement.

options.text: Continue living normally within the camp without restriction.
options.note: The text requires isolation outside the camp and visible signs of the person's condition, not normal, unrestricted living.

options.text: Serve as an assistant to the priest examining others.
options.note: No such role is described; the requirement is isolation and public warning, not priestly service.

options.text: No requirements are given.
options.note: Verses 45 to 46 give explicit requirements.

quiz.q: What must be done with a fabric or leather article contaminated with mildew that spreads after the priest's initial examination?

options.text: It must be burned, since the mildew is harmful.
options.note: Verses 51 to 52 explicitly require burning the article once the mildew is confirmed to have spread.

options.text: It must be given to the poor.
options.note: The text requires the contaminated article to be destroyed by fire, not redistributed to others.

options.text: It must be buried outside the camp.
options.note: The specified remedy is burning, not burial.

options.text: Nothing needs to be done with it.
options.note: Verses 51 to 52 explicitly require action.

quiz.q: How does Jesus' treatment of a leper in Mark 1:40-42 relate to the pattern established in Leviticus 13?

options.text: Jesus touches the leper and heals him, reversing the direction of defilement rather than becoming unclean Himself.
options.note: This reversal is central to how the Gospel account engages the Levitical pattern of isolation and uncleanness.

options.text: Jesus refuses to touch the leper, following the letter of the law exactly.
options.note: Mark 1:41 explicitly records Jesus touching the man, the opposite of refusing contact.

options.text: Jesus sends the leper away without any response.
options.note: Mark 1:41-42 records Jesus touching and healing the man, not dismissing him.

options.text: The Gospel account has no connection to this chapter.
options.note: The healing directly engages and reverses the pattern of isolation this chapter establishes.

questions: This chapter refused to let the priest make snap judgments; some things required patient, repeated examination over time. Where in your own life are you tempted to make a hasty judgment about someone or something that actually deserves more patience and care?

The diseased person had to publicly announce their own condition, unclean, unclean, rather than hide it. What is something in your life you have been hiding that you need to bring honestly into the light before God and trusted others?

Being cast outside the camp, away from community, was one of the heaviest consequences in this law. Have you ever felt isolated because of something you were struggling with, and how did that experience shape you?

Jesus touched lepers and made them clean, reversing what this entire chapter assumed about how defilement spreads. Where do you need to remember that Christ's holiness is not weakened by drawing near to your mess, but instead cleanses it?

Even ordinary household items, fabric and leather, could become contaminated and require careful attention. What ordinary areas of your life, unnoticed and unexamined, might need the same kind of honest inspection before God?

translationNotes.note: Infectious skin disease translates the Hebrew tsara'at, a broad term covering various skin conditions, often rendered leprosy in older translations though not identical to the modern medical diagnosis of Hansen's disease.

The requirement to cry out 'Unclean, unclean' served a public health function, warning others to keep their distance, while the torn clothes and loose hair were customary signs of mourning, marking the isolating grief of this condition.

The extension of tsara'at to describe mildew in fabric or leather shows the term's broader semantic range in Hebrew, encompassing a spreading, contaminating condition in materials as well as in human skin.
